judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports fully and correctly hit every expectation with equivalent depth and precise citations; they are comparable.

report_1.evidence: In PostgreSQL, **table owners bypass row-level security policies by default**, independent of the `BYPASSRLS` attribute — RLS policies are only enforced against the owner if the table is explicitly marked `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`. Migration `0003_rls.sql` never issues `ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`

report_2.evidence: In PostgreSQL, row security policies are **not applied to the table owner** unless the table is also marked `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` (which migration `0003_rls.sql` never does ... Currently, `.env.example`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `infra/main.tf` all set `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper`

report_1.evidence: It became exploitable in commit `b2a23d2` ("ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot") ... `b2a23d2` changed `.env.example`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `infra/main.tf` to: `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper`

report_2.evidence: It became exploitable as of commit **`b2a23d2`** — "ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot" ... all three deployment surfaces correctly pointed the runtime `DATABASE_URL` at `juniper_app:apppw@...`. `b2a23d2`'s diff changes exactly three lines

report_1.evidence: revert `juniper/.env.example:1`, `juniper/docker-compose.yml:5`, and `juniper/infra/main.tf:2` back to `juniper_app` ... add `ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` ... as a new migration

report_2.evidence: Revert the runtime `DATABASE_URL` in `.env.example`, `docker-compose.yml` (`api` service), and `infra/main.tf` back to the `juniper_app` role ... add `ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;`

report_1.evidence: Add an explicit `WHERE tenant_id = :tid` filter to the query in `juniper/backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19` (and any other tenant-scoped query) even though RLS should already enforce it

report_2.evidence: add an explicit `WHERE tenant_id = :tid` to the query in `backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19`, so a single point of DB misconfiguration (missing/bypassed RLS) doesn't unilaterally leak all tenants' data

report_1.evidence: Add a boot-time assertion in the API service ... that the connected DB role has no bypass privileges and is not the owner of any RLS-protected table ... and fail startup loudly if it's the owner or a bypass-capable role

report_2.evidence: Add a CI/startup assertion that fails fast if the runtime DB role has table ownership or `BYPASSRLS` ... queries `pg_roles.rolbypassrls` and `pg_tables` ownership for the connected role at app startup and refuses to boot
